serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a full bowl of Japanese rice topped with chicken soboro sauce and a half boiled egg as typically served in a Singapore Japanese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Half Boiled Egg Chicken Soboro Don — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g per 100 g) taken directly from HPB Foodo record for this dish. The HPB record reflects the protein-and-sauce component of the dish; the full bowl with rice will have substantially higher carbohydrate. Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sodium, saturated fat, sugar) estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite for ground chicken with soy-based sauce and mushroom (USDA SR Legacy #05064 ground chicken cooked, and USDA FoodData Central #748967 chicken soboro-style preparation) as HPB does not publish these fields for this record. Fat_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source for the sauce/protein component as recorded. See notes for zero-fat justification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken soboro don as a composite dish. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). The protein-and-sauce component (chicken soboro) contributes negligible carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB data (2.36 g/100 g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100. For a 350 g bowl, the rice component (~150 g cooked rice, ~35 g carbohydrate) gives GL ≈ (72 × 8.3) / 100 ≈ 6.0 using the blended carbohydrate of the full dish portion. This is a conservative estimate; actual GL of the full bowl with rice is substantially higher.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce, miso paste, and dashi; estimated ~327 mg sodium per 100 g of the sauce/protein component alone
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce and sauce served on the side, as the combined sodium from soy sauce, miso paste, and dashi in a full bowl is likely to exceed 600 mg per serving, which is a significant proportion of the 1500–2000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base carries a high glycemic index (~72) and the full bowl delivers a substantial glycemic load; mirin and sugar in soboro sauce add further refined carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice or a reduced rice portion to lower the glycemic load, and ask for reduced mirin and sugar in the soboro sauce, as the high-GI white rice base will cause a rapid postprandial blood glucose rise despite the protein-stabilising effect of the chicken and egg topping.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from soy sauce and dashi is the primary cardiovascular concern; the lean ground chicken and egg provide protein without significant saturated fat in the sauce component
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium preparation, as the high sodium content from soy sauce and dashi contributes to blood pressure elevation, while the lean chicken protein and mushroom fibre are otherwise compatible with a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean white meat chicken avoids arachidonic acid burden of red meat; ginger and mushroom provide anti-inflammatory gingerols and beta-glucans; high sodium from soy sauce may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of ginger and mushroom in this dish, but should request reduced-sodium soy sauce and miso to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling, and avoid alcohol which is pro-inflammatory and interacts with RA medications including methotrexate and biologics.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg Chicken Soboro Don is a Japanese rice bowl dish (donburi) consisting of seasoned ground chicken (soboro) cooked with diced shiitake mushrooms in a savoury sauce of soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, ginger, and dashi, served over steamed Japanese short-grain white rice and topped with a soft half-boiled egg. The soboro technique involves dry-frying minced chicken while stirring continuously to produce fine, crumbly, well-seasoned granules that absorb the sweet-savoury tare sauce. The dish has a tender, moist texture from the egg yolk contrasting with the fluffy rice and crumbly chicken topping. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Japanese restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, the HPB-recorded sauce and protein component is high in protein (10.7 g/100 g) and low in fat (0.0 g/100 g confirmed per HPB), but the full bowl with rice substantially increases carbohydrate and glycemic load. Sodium is elevated (~327 mg/100 g) from soy sauce and dashi, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The mushroom component contributes modest dietary fibre and beta-glucans with immune-modulating properties.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) are sourced directly from HPB Foodo with high confidence. However, fat_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for the sauce/protein component, which is unusual for a dish containing egg and chicken; this likely reflects the specific HPB measurement scope. Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sodium, sugar, potassium) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data for ground chicken with soy-based sauce, reducing confidence to medium. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo source for this specific record, which reflects the protein-and-sauce component of the dish as measured; the HPB data likely captures the soboro topping without the egg yolk fat contribution or reflects a specific preparation. This is documented as 'confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source'. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 consistent with the HPB fat_g of 0.0. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish preparation. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not available in HPB Foodo for this dish, and no sufficiently close USDA FoodData Central composite record exists for chicken soboro donburi as of 2026. The HPB nutrition data reflects the protein/sauce component only; the full bowl with rice has substantially higher carbohydrate, glycemic load, and caloric content. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database.
